chain_of_thought: Ghidra MCP backend is unreachable, so I cannot decompile decodeBytes or the JNI function, nor inspect exact arguments to strchr_default. I must fall back to reasoning from the crash metadata only.

The crash site is strchr_default, an internal implementation of strchr in the C library, called from decodeBytes inside libtbxml.so. Crashes in strchr usually stem from passing an invalid pointer or a non-terminated buffer; these are memory-safety issues if the pointer/length is derived from external input without validation.

The JNI bridge is Java_za_co_twyst_tbxml_TBXML_jniParse, called from TBXML.jniParse(byte[]) according to the stack trace. The Java call graph shows parse(String)->loadKml(String)->run()->jniParse(byte[]). The fuzzer harness also calls jniParse ultimately via fuzz_one_input, presumably giving arbitrary byte arrays.

decodeBytes suggests it decodes the input bytes (XML) into internal structures, probably using strlen/strchr-like parsing for tags. If strchr_default crashes, common causes include: (1) input pointer not pointing to accessible memory (e.g., after freeing or miscomputing an offset), or (2) not ensuring NUL-termination before using strchr on a buffer that might not be terminated within bounds. Either implies a code defect in libtbxml rather than simply an allocator or sanitizer stopping the program.

Is this reachable under realistic app usage? The call path from TBXML.parse(String) and plugin.google.maps.PluginKmlOverlay.loadKml(String) indicates local or remotely fetched KML strings are parsed through this exact JNI. KML is often obtained from files, network, or app data, which are at least partially attacker-controlled in many apps (e.g., loading overlays from a URL). Even if in this app it might be more constrained, there is no evidence of strict validation on the Java side before passing data to jniParse(byte[]).

The fuzz harness does not appear to introduce unrealistic conditions such as NULL JNIEnv or impossible object lifetimes; it just supplies arbitrary byte arrays into jniParse. Passing malformed KML or arbitrary bytes to a native XML parser should not cause a libc strchr crash; a robust parser should handle arbitrary bytes with bounds checks and proper lengths. Therefore the crash indicates a likely memory-safety bug in how decodeBytes uses strchr on fuzzer-controlled data.

Without the decompiled code, I cannot identify the exact arithmetic or missing bounds check, but the combination of: (a) crash inside strchr_default (not a deliberate abort), (b) native XML/KML decoder using raw C string APIs, and (c) direct data flow from untrusted byte[] into decodeBytes, strongly suggests an out-of-bounds read/write or invalid pointer dereference based on malformed input.

Given this, I classify as a likely genuine vulnerability. Impact is at least high (memory corruption / potential code execution), though exploitability details (heap vs stack, exact corruption) are unknown. Confidence is reduced slightly because I cannot confirm the source of the invalid pointer or whether the libc implementation itself raised a defensive abort, but allocator/sanitizer frames are not mentioned—only strchr_default is top native frame.

Affected library is libtbxml.so; the vulnerable function is decodeBytes or lower-level helpers it calls that use strchr/strlen on attacker-controlled buffers. The JNI entry is Java_za_co_twyst_tbxml_TBXML_jniParse. Recommendations: review decodeBytes and related parsing code for unchecked uses of strchr/strlen/memcpy, enforce length-based parsing instead of relying on NUL termination, and add robust validation around buffer boundaries.
reasons: Crash occurs in strchr_default called from decodeBytes in libtbxml.so, indicating misuse of C string APIs rather than a controlled abort.
Input to decodeBytes ultimately comes from TBXML.jniParse(byte[]) which receives attacker-influenced data (KML/XML) via TBXML.parse(String) and plugin.google.maps.PluginKmlOverlay.loadKml(String).

Fuzzing arbitrary byte arrays into jniParse should not legitimately crash libc; a robust parser would validate lengths and handle malformed data, so this indicates likely memory-safety issues such as invalid pointer or out-of-bounds read/write.

evidence.note: Crash in libc strchr implementation when called from decodeBytes suggests invalid pointer or string without proper bounds or terminator, derived from input bytes.
JNI method processes a byte[] from Java without visible Java-side validation; it is the native entrypoint through which fuzzer passes arbitrary data.
Application code feeds KML/XML strings into TBXML native parser; such data can plausibly be attacker-controlled (network/file-based overlays).

recommendations: Audit decodeBytes and related TBXML parsing routines for unsafe uses of strchr/strlen/memcpy on attacker-controlled buffers; replace with length-bounded operations and explicit checks.
Ensure JNI layer validates input byte[] / String lengths and content before invoking native parsing to avoid invalid pointers or missing terminators.
Add defensive assertions and fuzz tests around KML/XML parsing to reproduce and then fix the specific invalid-pointer scenario causing strchr_default to crash.
assumptions: KML/XML or other data passed to TBXML.parse/String and jniParse(byte[]) can be influenced by external input such as files or network.
The crash is not due to an injected harness behavior like forcing a NULL buffer that real app code would never pass.
strchr_default belongs to the standard C library and is reliable, so its crash indicates misuse by caller decodeBytes rather than an internal libc bug.
limitations: Unable to decompile libtbxml.so due to MCP backend connection failure; exact faulty code pattern in decodeBytes is inferred, not observed.
Java-side code for TBXML and PluginKmlOverlay was not directly inspected; attacker-control assessment is based on typical usage patterns of KML overlays.
No register/memory dump from the crash; cannot distinguish between out-of-bounds read, write, or pure invalid-pointer dereference in strchr_default.

chain_of_thought: Ghidra MCP is unreachable, so I can’t decompile decodeBytes or the exact call site of strpbrk, but I know from the stack that the first app-native frame below libc is decodeBytes in libtbxml.so, called from the JNI entry Java_za_co_twyst_tbxml_TBXML_jniParse.

The process termination symbol is strpbrk, which is a libc string-scanning function that walks a char* until a terminator or a matching char from a set is found. Crashes in strpbrk are almost always due to an invalid pointer (NULL or non-mapped) or it walking off into unmapped memory because the buffer is not properly terminated or the pointer is corrupted.

The library map shows strpbrk and decodeBytes are in libtbxml.so, meaning decodeBytes almost certainly calls the imported libc strpbrk on some buffer derived from the XML bytes passed down from Java. That’s classic parsing code (TBXML is a well-known tiny XML parser).

The JNI bridge TBXML.jniParse(byte[]):long receives a byte[] from Java. The Java call graph shows the path from TBXML.parse(String) → PluginKmlOverlay.loadKml(String) → … → TBXML.jniParse(byte[]). The KML string is app data that plausibly comes from network, file, or user-provided KML overlay URLs. So attacker control over the XML/KML contents is realistic in normal app usage.

I can’t see the actual C code, but TBXML-like decoders usually convert from UTF-8 bytes to an internal C string buffer (decodeBytes), then scan using libc functions (strpbrk, strchr, etc.) without explicit length parameters. If decodeBytes miscalculates length, fails to null-terminate, or assumes the buffer has a ‘\0’ while working directly on a raw bytes array from Java, strpbrk will read past the end until it hits unmapped memory, producing a crash. This matches a classic out-of-bounds read based on malformed input.

Because the fuzzer is exercising jniParse directly, the crash may be hit with highly malformed bytes (including embedded NULs, missing terminators, etc.), but those are still realistic for a byte[] coming from decoded network/file input; there’s nothing in the Java side indicating that it validates encoding or enforces that the array contains a proper C-string-terminated buffer before passing it to native.

There’s no evidence that the crash is due solely to an artificial harness quirk such as passing NULL pointers or impossible JNI arguments: the JNI signature matches, and the fuzz harness just routes arbitrary byte[] to the same JNI function that production Java uses. Therefore, the most plausible explanation is a real memory safety bug in decodeBytes’ use of strpbrk when parsing attacker-controlled XML/KML content.

I can’t assign a specific CWE with absolute certainty without seeing code, but the pattern is consistent with unchecked traversal of a buffer as a C string (CWE-787 Out-of-bounds Read/Write, or CWE-119 family). The presence of a direct libc strpbrk frame and a parser named decodeBytes immediately below strongly suggests missing bounds checks or termination handling.

Given the realistic taint from Java input to native, the crash dynamics, and the typical TBXML implementation style, I assess this as a likely genuine vulnerability with moderate-to-high confidence, and at least high severity (attacker-controlled memory corruption / DoS in a parser that may be invoked on untrusted KML).
reasons: Crash occurs inside libc strpbrk called by decodeBytes in libtbxml.so, indicating misuse of C string scanning on native buffers.
Input buffer to decodeBytes ultimately comes from TBXML.jniParse(byte[]), which receives attacker-controlled bytes from Java without evident native-side validation.
Pattern is consistent with XML/KML parser using unbounded C string operations on fuzzed/attacker data, leading to out-of-bounds read and process termination, not an obvious harness-only misuse.

evidence.snippet:
decodeBytes(...)
    ...
    strpbrk(buf, charset); // top frame in crash, buf derived from jniParse byte[]
evidence.note: strpbrk implies scanning of a C string; when buf is not guaranteed NUL-terminated or within bounds, this can read past the end of attacker-controlled data.
Arguments come directly from Java byte[], which is ultimately derived from external KML/XML strings.
Native JNI method taking arbitrary bytes; fuzz harness and Java call graph show it is reachable with untrusted input.

recommendations: Review and harden decodeBytes to use length-aware APIs (e.g., memchr/strnlen) and ensure all buffers passed to strpbrk or other string functions are properly bounded and NUL-terminated.
Add explicit length validation at the JNI boundary (jniParse) to reject overly long or malformed byte[] inputs before passing them into the native TBXML parser.
Create regression tests with malformed and boundary-case KML/XML inputs to reproduce this crash and verify fixes under ASan/Valgrind or similar tools.
assumptions: decodeBytes takes the jniParse byte[] contents more or less directly as the buffer passed to strpbrk.
The Java KML/XML input can be influenced by an attacker (e.g., via remote URLs or user-supplied files).
limitations: Could not decompile native libtbxml.so functions due to MCP tool connectivity failure; conclusions are based on symbol names and stack trace only.
Exact nature of memory corruption (read vs. write, overflow extent) is inferred, not directly observed from disassembly.
